serving.natural_language_portion: One restaurant serving of charcoal linguine aglio olio with prawns is approximately 350 g, comprising a full plate of pasta with a generous portion of sautéed prawns.

nutrient_source.title: Pasta, cooked, enriched, without added salt (USDA SR Legacy #20124) combined with Shrimp, cooked (USDA SR Legacy #15152) and Olive oil (USDA SR Legacy #04053); aglio olio preparation estimated from component proportions
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for charcoal linguine aglio olio with prawns exists in any approved primary source. Values estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data: cooked pasta (~65% by weight), prawns (~20%), olive oil (~8%), garlic and chilli (~7%). Charcoal (activated carbon) additive contributes negligible macronutrients. Weighted composite calculation applied. Sodium elevated to reflect restaurant-level seasoning.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; pasta (spaghetti/linguine, white, boiled) GI approximately 45–49
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for charcoal linguine aglio olio with prawns. GI of 45 assigned based on University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021, Am J Clin Nutr) data for white pasta (linguine/spaghetti, boiled al dente), which consistently falls in the 44–49 range. Charcoal additive does not materially alter glycemic response. Protein and fat from prawns and olive oil further attenuate postprandial glucose rise. GL per serving = (45 × 19.5 × 3.5) / 100 = 30.7 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from restaurant-level seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A single 350 g restaurant serving provides approximately 1,470 mg sodium (~64% of the 2,300 mg daily limit), making this dish a significant sodium load for individuals managing hypertension; portion reduction and requesting reduced salt are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from refined pasta carbohydrates, partially offset by protein and fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated glycemic load of ~31 g per serving is moderate; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to approximately 150–200 g, pair with additional non-starchy vegetables, and monitor postprandial glucose response given the refined wheat base of the linguine.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate dietary cholesterol from prawns, partially offset by heart-healthy olive oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The olive oil base provides beneficial monounsaturated fats, but the high sodium content and moderate cholesterol from prawns (~238 mg per serving) warrant caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease; choosing a smaller portion and requesting less salt is recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Charcoal Linguine Aglio Olio with Prawns, marketed as 'Black Sea' at Pasta Fresca Da Salvatore in Singapore, is an Italian pasta dish featuring linguine coloured and flavoured with activated charcoal or squid ink, tossed in a classic aglio olio sauce of extra-virgin olive oil, thinly sliced garlic, and dried peperoncino (chilli flakes), and finished with sautéed prawns. The charcoal linguine gives the dish its distinctive jet-black appearance and a subtle earthy undertone, while the aglio olio base delivers a fragrant, garlicky, and mildly spicy flavour profile. The prawns add sweetness, umami depth, and a firm, succulent texture. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-energy meal (~175 kcal per 100 g) with a meaningful protein contribution from the prawns (~9.2 g per 100 g), heart-healthy monounsaturated fats from olive oil, and a moderate glycemic index (~45) due to the pasta base. However, restaurant preparation typically results in high sodium levels (~420 mg per 100 g), which is a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is dairy-free and contains gluten and crustacean allergens. It is suitable for non-vegetarian diets and is commonly served as a main course in Italian restaurants across Singapore.

confidence_reason: No direct nutritional entry for this specific dish exists in any approved primary source. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for cooked pasta, cooked prawns, and olive oil using weighted proportions. The charcoal additive contributes negligible macronutrients. GI is analogously estimated from University of Sydney GI Database data for white pasta. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved databases and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. This dish is a restaurant menu item ('Black Sea') from Pasta Fresca Da Salvatore, Boat Quay, Singapore, available on field survey. The canonical English name 'Charcoal Linguine Aglio Olio with Prawns' is used in place of the menu marketing name 'Black Sea' for food database purposes. 2. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data; no direct approved-source entry exists for this dish. 3. Sodium is elevated to ~420 mg/100 g to reflect restaurant-level seasoning typical of aglio olio preparations. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries used for this composite estimation. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0; olive oil and prawn-based preparations contain negligible industrially produced trans fats — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central component data. 6. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for white pasta (linguine/spaghetti, boiled); no direct GI measurement exists for charcoal linguine aglio olio with prawns. 7. Multilingual local names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are not applicable for this Italian dish; the cuisine is Italian with origin in Italy, so Singaporean multilingual naming rules do not apply. 8. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and are provided as best-effort composite values.
